questions.text: On a rhythm strip, the R-R interval varies by more than 4 small boxes. The rhythm is:
Which of the following is the intrinsic rate of the AV junction?
ECG hallmarks of a sinus-origin rhythm include all of the following EXCEPT:

answers.text: Positive P wave before each QRS
Inverted P waves in leads II, III, and aVF

questions.text: What is the rate range for Normal Sinus Rhythm?
Sinus bradycardia is best defined as a sinus rhythm with a rate of:
Which of the following is the LEAST likely cause of sinus bradycardia in a healthy adult?

answers.text: Resting state in a person under 25

questions.text: A patient has a regular sinus rhythm with a rate of 130 bpm that began gradually during exercise. The most likely diagnosis is:
In respiratory sinus arrhythmia, the heart rate:

answers.text: Decreases with inspiration and increases with expiration
Increases gradually with inspiration and decreases with expiration
Does not vary with the respiratory cycle

questions.text: On a rhythm strip you see a pause where the next expected PQRST never appears, and the pause length is NOT a multiple of the underlying P-P interval. This is most consistent with:
Which condition is a known cause of sinus arrest / pause?
A premature P wave that differs in shape from sinus P waves and interrupts the underlying rhythm is characteristic of:
Three or more premature beats in a row is called:
Every other beat is a premature beat. This pattern is called:
Multiformed Atrial Rhythm (MAR) requires at least how many different P-wave configurations?
Multiformed Atrial Tachycardia (MAT) is most classically associated with which condition?
What is the rate range for Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT)?
The term 'paroxysmal' is used to describe a rhythm that:
Of the three SVT mechanisms, which involves an impulse traveling through an ectopic accessory pathway and causing pre-excitation of the ventricles?
The classic ECG triad of Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome is:

answers.text: Saw-tooth waves, irregular rhythm, narrow QRS
Delta wave, short PR interval, widened QRS
Inverted P, no PR interval, narrow QRS
Tall peaked T, prolonged QT, wide QRS

questions.text: Saw-toothed 'flutter waves' on the baseline are the ECG hallmark of:
What is the typical atrial rate in atrial flutter?
An 'irregularly irregular' ventricular rhythm with a fibrillatory baseline and no identifiable P waves is most consistent with:
Atrial fibrillation increases the risk of which of the following due to pooling of blood in the atria?
Atrial fibrillation is considered 'controlled' when the ventricular rate is:
The first-line drug for treating narrow-complex SVT is:
Anticoagulation is indicated in atrial fibrillation that has been present for at least:
When the AV junction paces the heart and a P wave is visible, it is most often:

answers.text: Upright in leads II, III, and aVF
Inverted in leads II, III, and aVF

questions.text: When a P wave precedes the QRS in a junctional beat, the PR interval is typically:
What distinguishes a junctional escape beat from a premature junctional complex (PJC)?

answers.text: An escape beat occurs LATE (after the next expected sinus beat); a PJC occurs EARLY
An escape beat has a wide QRS; a PJC has a narrow QRS
An escape beat has an upright P wave; a PJC has an inverted P wave
An escape beat originates in the ventricles; a PJC originates in the atria

questions.text: What is the rate range for a junctional escape rhythm?
What is the rate range for an accelerated junctional rhythm?
Junctional tachycardia is defined by a junctional rhythm with a rate of:
Which medication or condition is a common cause of junctional escape rhythm?
The recommended first-line intervention for a slow junctional rhythm causing dizziness or syncope is:
Which finding most strongly distinguishes Wolff-Parkinson-White from a typical SVT?

answers.text: A delta wave with a widened QRS
Inverted P waves in lead II
